title: Getting Started with networkR
text: vignette walks every stage networkR pipeline using fully synthetic data mimics multi-tissue malaria vaccine immunology study. real data files needed — mock panels generated inline written temporary directory. end : generated inspected raw long-format flow-cytometry panels; configured pipeline two tissue compartments two stratifications; traced data imputation, discretization, blacklist construction; learned plotted Bayesian network structure full cohort; compared network structure exposure groups (Malaria Preexposed vs. Naive) protection outcomes (Protected vs. protected).

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: Bayesian Network (BN) probabilistic graphical model whose structure directed acyclic graph (DAG). node XiX_i represents variable; directed edge Xj→XiX_j \X_i encodes XjX_j direct probabilistic predictor XiX_i conditioning parents. joint distribution factorises along graph: P(X1,…,Xn)=∏=1nP(Xi∣pa(Xi))P(X_1, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{=1}^{n} P\!\left(X_i \mid \text{pa}(X_i)\right) pa(Xi)\text{pa}(X_i) parent set XiX_i. Missing edges denote conditional independence: Xi⊥⊥Xj∣{Xk:k≠,j}X_i \perp\!\!\!\perp X_j \mid \{X_k : k \ne , j\}. makes BNs useful teasing apart direct indirect associations high-dimensional immunological data.

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: networkR formalises tier system encodes causal precedence variable classes. Arcs run lower-priority tier higher-priority tier blacklisted structure learning begins. Three blacklist components constructed automatically: Measurement → Exogenous — cellular proportion can cause design variable. Exogenous → Exogenous — design factors mutually independent construction (factorial design assumption; configurable). Cross-tier (high tier → low tier) — e.g. PBMC cells (tier 3) parents Liver cells (tier 2). Compartment identity encoded structurally measurement column prefixes measurementGroups definitions, separate row-level Tissue node. lets network learn within- cross-compartment relationships paired subject-level measurements still using measurement groups node colouring tier-aware blacklist construction.

what: 1.3 Bootstrap stability and the averaged network
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: data finite, single structure-learning run sensitive sampling noise. networkR uses bootstrap resampling (Friedman et al. 1999): RR bootstrap datasets drawn network learned . arc stability edge →BA \B fraction bootstrap networks contain →BA \Bor B→AB \(direction assessed separately direction field boot.strength()). averaged network retains arcs whose stability exceeds threshold (default 0.40 real studies; use 0.20 small demo dataset).

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: Raw bootstrap strength unsigned. networkR multiplies sign Pearson correlation two nodes continuous (pre-discretization) data, giving signed strength ss: sAB=sign(rAB)×p̂ABs_{AB} = \text{sign}(r_{AB}) \times \hat{p}_{AB} p̂AB\hat{p}_{AB} bootstrap arc probability rABr_{AB} Pearson rr. delta Δ=sgroup1−sgroup2\Delta = s_{\text{group}_1} - s_{\text{group}_2} summarises directional enrichment edge two cohorts.

previous_headings: 2. Mock data: a simulated malaria vaccine study
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: simulated cohort 24 subjects divided three experimental groups, measured two tissue compartments (Liver PBMC) single timepoint (D1). yields 24 rows joined subject table ReadPanels() — one row per subject, Liver PBMC features occupying separate column groups. Simulated study design (24 subjects total) Subjects S01–S08 Malaria Preexposed (vaccinated); S09–S16 Malaria Naive (vaccinated); S17–S24 Control Animals (unvaccinated, protection outcome).

previous_headings: 2. Mock data: a simulated malaria vaccine study
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: simulate cell-type proportions using Dirichlet distribution. group-specific concentration parameters 𝛂\boldsymbol{\alpha} encode biological signal: Preexposed animals relatively elevated Kupffer cells (Liver) CD8 T cells (PBMC), Control Animals flatter distributions consistent naive systemic immune state.
code: # Dirichlet draw for a single observation. # Exploits: if G_i ~ Gamma(alpha_i, 1) then G / sum(G) ~ Dirichlet(alpha). rdirichlet_one <- function(alpha) {   raw <- vapply(alpha, function(a) stats::rgamma(1, shape = a, rate = 1), numeric(1))   raw / sum(raw) }  # Build a long-format panel table (one row per subject × cell type). make_panel_long <- function(cell_types, alpha_by_group,                              subjects, groups, protection, seed) {   set.seed(seed)   rows <- lapply(seq_along(subjects), function(i) {     fracs <- rdirichlet_one(alpha_by_group[[groups[i]]])     data.frame(       SubjectId       = subjects[i],       TimepointLabel2 = "D1",       GroupName       = groups[i],       Protection      = protection[i],       CellType        = cell_types,       Fraction        = fracs,       stringsAsFactors = FALSE     )   })   do.call(rbind, rows) }

previous_headings: 2. Mock data: a simulated malaria vaccine study
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: Five liver cell types simulated. Dirichlet 𝛂\boldsymbol{\alpha} shapes differ group: Preexposed animals higher α\alpha Kupffer Cells (resident macrophages capture malaria parasites), Naive Control animals show uniform distributions. First 10 rows raw Liver panel (long format)
code: # Subject metadata n_subjects      <- 24 subject_ids     <- sprintf("S%02d", 1:n_subjects) group_labels    <- rep(c("Malaria Preexposed", "Malaria Naive", "Control Animal"), each = 8) protection_raw  <- c(   rep(c("Yes", "No"), each = 4),  # S01-S08 Preexposed: 4 protected, 4 not   rep(c("Yes", "No"), each = 4),  # S09-S16 Naive: 4 protected, 4 not   rep("No", 8)                    # S17-S24 Controls: placeholder → overwritten to NA )  # Liver cell types and group-specific Dirichlet alpha vectors liver_cell_types <- c(   "Kupffer Cells", "CD8 T Cells", "NK Cells",   "Gamma Delta T Cells", "Conventional DC" ) liver_alpha <- list(   "Malaria Preexposed" = c(5.0, 2.0, 2.0, 1.0, 0.8),  # elevated Kupffer + moderate NK   "Malaria Naive"      = c(2.0, 3.0, 3.0, 1.5, 0.5),  # elevated CD8 / NK   "Control Animal"     = c(1.0, 1.0, 2.5, 1.0, 0.5)   # relatively flat; more NK )  liver_panel <- make_panel_long(   liver_cell_types, liver_alpha, subject_ids, group_labels, protection_raw, seed = 42 ) # Add ~10 % random missing values to simulate instrument dropouts set.seed(7) liver_panel$Fraction[sample(nrow(liver_panel), floor(nrow(liver_panel) * 0.10))] <- NA  cat("Liver panel: ", nrow(liver_panel), "rows × ", ncol(liver_panel), "columns\n") #> Liver panel:  120 rows ×  6 columns cat("Missing Fraction values:", sum(is.na(liver_panel$Fraction)), "\n") #> Missing Fraction values: 12

previous_headings: 2. Mock data: a simulated malaria vaccine study
title: Getting Started with networkR
code: pbmc_cell_types <- c(   "CD14 Monocytes", "CD4 T Cells", "CD8 T Cells", "NK Cells", "B Cells" ) pbmc_alpha <- list(   "Malaria Preexposed" = c(2.0, 2.0, 4.5, 2.0, 0.5),  # strong CD8 enrichment   "Malaria Naive"      = c(3.5, 3.0, 2.0, 2.0, 0.5),  # monocyte / CD4 skew   "Control Animal"     = c(4.5, 2.0, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5)   # monocyte dominated )  pbmc_panel <- make_panel_long(   pbmc_cell_types, pbmc_alpha, subject_ids, group_labels, protection_raw, seed = 99 ) set.seed(8) pbmc_panel$Fraction[sample(nrow(pbmc_panel), floor(nrow(pbmc_panel) * 0.10))] <- NA  cat("PBMC panel:", nrow(pbmc_panel), "rows × ", ncol(pbmc_panel), "columns\n") #> PBMC panel: 120 rows ×  6 columns cat("Missing Fraction values:", sum(is.na(pbmc_panel$Fraction)), "\n") #> Missing Fraction values: 12

previous_headings: 2. Mock data: a simulated malaria vaccine study
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: ReadPanels() discovers files globbing dataPath filenames matching filePattern. tissue compartment parsed filename: file containing "liver" (case-insensitive) maps canonical label "Liver".
code: panel_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "networkR_vignette_panels") dir.create(panel_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE) utils::write.csv(liver_panel, file.path(panel_dir, "liver_panel.csv"), row.names = FALSE) utils::write.csv(pbmc_panel,  file.path(panel_dir, "pbmc_panel.csv"),  row.names = FALSE) cat("Panel files written to:", panel_dir, "\n") #> Panel files written to: /tmp/RtmpmNqYjw/networkR_vignette_panels list.files(panel_dir) #> [1] "liver_panel.csv" "pbmc_panel.csv"

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: networkR configured entirely named R list produced BuildConfiguration() (equivalently ParseConfiguration() YAML file). configuration controls every stage pipeline:

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: override parameters differ package defaults. Key demo choices: 50 bootstrap replicates averageNetworkThreshold = 0.2 ensure non-trivial averaged network small dataset. real study use R≥1000R \ge 1000 replicates averageNetworkThreshold = 0.4. YAML file equivalent available system.file("configuration_template.yml", package = "networkR").
code: configuration <- BuildConfiguration(list(   study = list(     studyName  = "Malaria Vaccine Immunology — Demo Study",     randomSeed = 42   ),   data = list(     dataPath    = panel_dir,     filePattern = "[.](csv)$"   ),   variables = list(     measurementGroups = list(       list(         groupName      = "liverCells",         columnPrefixes = list("Liver_"),         tier           = 2,         fillColour     = "lightgreen",         borderColour   = "darkgreen"       ),       list(         groupName      = "pbmcCells",         columnPrefixes = list("PBMC_"),         tier           = 3,         fillColour     = "lightblue",         borderColour   = "steelblue"       )     )   ),   imputation = list(     numberOfImputations = 3,     maximumIterations   = 5   ),   structureLearning = list(     algorithms                 = list("tabu"),     selectedBootstrapAlgorithm = "tabu",     bootstrapReplicates        = 50,     averageNetworkThreshold    = 0.2   ),   stratifications = list(     list(       analysisName = "exposure",       variableName = "GroupName",       levels       = list("Malaria Preexposed", "Malaria Naive"),       filters      = list()     ),     list(       analysisName = "protection",       variableName = "Protection",       levels       = list("Yes", "No"),       filters      = list(         list(column = "VaccinationStatus", operator = "equals",    value = "Vaccinated"),         list(column = "Protection",        operator = "notMissing")       )     )   ),   output = list(savePlots = FALSE) ))  print(configuration) #> networkR configuration #>   Study name: Malaria Vaccine Immunology — Demo Study #>   Data path: /tmp/RtmpmNqYjw/networkR_vignette_panels #>   Exogenous variables: GroupName, VaccinationStatus, Timepoint #>   Measurement groups: liverCells, pbmcCells

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: RunPipeline() one-call entry point. executes complete chain — ingestion, derived-variable annotation, imputation, discretization, full-model structure learning, stratified sub-models, pairwise structure comparisons — returns single networkRPipelineResult object containing every intermediate result plot. remainder vignette unpacks component pipeline_result detail.
code: set.seed(42) pipeline_result <- RunPipeline(configuration) print(pipeline_result) #> networkR pipeline result #>   Study name: Malaria Vaccine Immunology — Demo Study #>   Rows: 24 #>   Measurement columns: 10 #>   Stratified analyses: 2

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: ReadPanels() discovers files configuration$data$dataPath whose names match configuration$data$filePattern. file : Reads raw long-format table (xlsx csv). Parses tissue compartment filename using configuration$data$tissueMap. Renames TimepointLabel2 → Timepoint (whatever rawTimepointColumn/analysisTimepointColumn mapping specifies). Normalises CellType strings valid column names prefixes {Tissue}_{cellTypePrefix} (e.g. "Kupffer Cells" → "Liver_Pct_Kupffer_Cells"). Pivots long wide, producing one subject × timepoint row per tissue panel. individual pivoted tables full_join-ed shared key columns (SubjectId, Timepoint, Protection, GroupName). tissue identity already lives measurement column prefixes, join produces one row per subject Liver_ PBMC_ columns aligned side side. Missing values join therefore reflect genuine assay dropouts rather structural cross-tissue design artifact.
code: # The individual functions — equivalent to what RunPipeline calls internally: subject_table <- ReadPanels(configuration) subject_table <- AddDerivedVariables(subject_table, configuration)

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: AddDerivedVariables() applies two rules defined configuration$variables$derivedVariables: VaccinationStatus (ifEquals): set "Unvaccinated" GroupName == "Control Animal", otherwise "Vaccinated". Protection (replaceTargetWhenEquals): overwrite Protection NA GroupName == "Control Animal". raw CSV value controls irrelevant.

Protection distribution:

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: missing values measurement columns imputed structure learning. networkR uses Multiple Imputation Chained Equations (MICE) predictive mean matching (method = "pmm" default). PMM preserves empirical marginal distribution variable: imputed value always real observed value donor matched predicted values. joined analysis table now subject-level, compartments given subject sit row. MICE therefore uses real paired cross-compartment measurements predictors filling assay dropouts, rather reconstructing entire missing tissue block row-level Tissue indicator. MICE fails (e.g. rows relative predictors small sub-cohort), ImputeMeasurements() automatically falls back column-wise median imputation emits warning.
code: # Equivalent single-step call: imputation_result <- ImputeMeasurements(pipeline_result$subjectTable, configuration)

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: continuousAnalysisData slot holds imputed continuous values (used later computing signed arc strengths via Pearson correlations). imputedData slot holds values plus subject-level metadata columns.
code:
ir <- pipeline_result$imputationResult  cat("Measurement columns imputed (", length(ir$measurementColumns), "):
", sep = "") #> Measurement columns imputed (10): cat(paste(ir$measurementColumns, collapse = "

") #> NAs before imputation: 0 cat("NAs after  imputation:", n_na_after,  "

") #> Stratifier variables (not imputed): Protection

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: Bayesian network score functions categorical variables require discrete (factor) inputs. networkR uses Hartemink’s pairwise mutual-information algorithm (bnlearn::discretize(method = "hartemink")) choose cut points maximise pairwise mutual information across pairs measurement variables jointly. preferable per-column quantile binning sensitive covariance structure data. algorithm requires sufficient within-cell counts initial qq-quantile preprocessing pass (initialBreaks = 20 default). Columns satisfy requirement (distinct values rows) routed quantile-based fallback (fallbackBreaks = 2), simply splits median.
code: # Equivalent: discretization_result <- DiscretizeMeasurements(imputation_result, configuration)

", sep = "")   cat(paste(dr$fallbackColumns, collapse = "
"), "
") } else {   cat("Fallback columns: none — all columns passed the Hartemink pre-check.
") } #> Fallback columns (5): #> Liver_Pct_CD8_T_Cells #> Liver_Pct_Conventional_DC #> Liver_Pct_NK_Cells #> PBMC_Pct_CD4_T_Cells #> PBMC_Pct_CD8_T_Cells

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: discretization every measurement column ordered factor finalBreaks = 3 levels default. Hartemink algorithm determines cut points jointly; levels labelled 1, 2, 3 (low high within column’s marginal distribution). Factor levels first three measurement columns First 8 rows discrete data (design + first three measurement columns)
code: first_three <- dr$measurementColumns[1:3] lvl_display <- lapply(first_three, function(cn) {   x <- dr$discreteData[[cn]]   data.frame(     variable = cn,     `n levels` = nlevels(x),     levels = paste(levels(x), collapse = " / "),     check.names = FALSE   ) }) knitr::kable(do.call(rbind, lvl_display), caption = "Factor levels for first three measurement columns")

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: BuildBlacklist() assembles data.frame forbidden arcs (, ) three sources: Measurement → Exogenous: Every arc measurement column active exogenous variable forbidden (cellular proportions cause group assignments). Exogenous → Exogenous: Arcs exogenous variables forbidden (factorial design assumption — modify exogenous variables correlated). Tier cross-arcs: Arcs measurement group AA measurement group BB forbidden whenever tier()>tier(B)\text{tier}() > \text{tier}(B) — .e. downstream-tissue cells (PBMC, tier 3) parents upstream-tissue cells (Liver, tier 2).
code: # Equivalent: blacklist_result <- BuildBlacklist(discretization_result, configuration)

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: First 15 rows blacklist (→ arcs exist)
code:
bl <- pipeline_result$fullModelResult$blacklist  cat("Total blacklisted arcs:", nrow(bl), "

") #> Total blacklisted arcs: 47  # Breakdown by arc category is_exo_src <- bl$from %in% pipeline_result$fullModelResult$activeExogenousVariables is_exo_tgt <- bl$to   %in% pipeline_result$fullModelResult$activeExogenousVariables  cat("Arcs blocked: measurement → exogenous: ",     sum(!is_exo_src &  is_exo_tgt), "
",     "Arcs blocked: exogenous → exogenous:  ",     sum( is_exo_src &  is_exo_tgt), "
",     "Arcs blocked: high-tier → low-tier:   ",     sum(!is_exo_src & !is_exo_tgt), "
", sep = "") #> Arcs blocked: measurement → exogenous: 20 #> Arcs blocked: exogenous → exogenous:  2 #> Arcs blocked: high-tier → low-tier:   25

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: networkR supports Hill-Climbing ("hillClimbing") Tabu search ("tabu") score-based BN structure learning. Tabu search augments greedy hill-climbing recency memory (tabu list) forbids recently reversed moves, helping escape local optima. algorithms optimise BIC score discrete data default. learning single best-scoring network full dataset, networkR runs bootstrap resampling: draws RR datasets replacement re-runs Tabu search , aggregates arc frequencies boot.strength table.
code: # Equivalent: structure_result <- LearnBayesianNetwork(discretization_result, configuration)

title: Getting Started with networkR
code: sr <- pipeline_result$fullModelResult cat("BIC scores by algorithm:\n") #> BIC scores by algorithm: print(round(sr$networkScores, 2)) #>    tabu  #> -249.39

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: row bootstrapStrength table directed arc (→ ) estimated stability (strength) direction probability (direction). sort stability descending see robustly supported edges. Top 15 arcs bootstrap stability (strength = fraction bootstrap networks containing arc)
code: bs <- as.data.frame(sr$bootstrapStrength) bs <- bs[order(-bs$strength), ] knitr::kable(   head(bs, 15),   digits = 3,   row.names = FALSE,   caption = "Top 15 arcs by bootstrap stability (strength = fraction of bootstrap networks containing the arc)" )

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: averaged network retains arcs whose stability exceeds averageNetworkThreshold (0.20). network visualized compared downstream.
code:
cat("Averaged network threshold:", sr$threshold, "

") #> Averaged network threshold: 0.2 avg_arcs <- bnlearn::arcs(sr$averagedNetwork) cat("Arcs in the averaged network (", nrow(avg_arcs), "):
", sep = "") #> Arcs in the averaged network (17): if (nrow(avg_arcs) > 0) {   knitr::kable(avg_arcs, row.names = FALSE) } else {   cat("No arcs exceeded the threshold — try lowering averageNetworkThreshold.
") }

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: PlotBayesianNetwork() renders averaged network using ggraph Fruchterman-Reingold ("fr") force-directed layout. Node colours encode membership measurement group exogenous category; edge width transparency encode bootstrap stability. Node colour scheme demo:  Wider edges opaque edges indicate higher bootstrap stability. node layout stochastic (force-directed); run set.seed() PlotBayesianNetwork() reproducible layout.
code: pipeline_result$plotObjects$fullModel

what: 11.1 Exposure stratification: Malaria Preexposed vs. Naive
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: RunPipeline() runs separate BN stratum defined configuration$stratifications. "exposure" stratification, rows partitioned GroupName "Malaria Preexposed" "Malaria Naive" sub-cohorts (8 subjects = 8 rows ).
code: pipeline_result$plotObjects$stratifiedModels$exposure$levelPlots[["Malaria Preexposed"]] pipeline_result$plotObjects$stratifiedModels$exposure$levelPlots[["Malaria Naive"]]

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: comparison scatter plot places signed bootstrap strength Group 1 (Malaria Preexposed) y-axis Group 2 (Malaria Naive) x-axis. point one edge union groups’ bootstrap tables. identity line (y=xy = x): equally strong groups. identity line: stronger (different direction) Preexposed. identity line: stronger Naive. Points |Δ|>|\Delta| >edgeLabelThreshold labelled. Note 8 subjects per stratum, many edges low similar bootstrap strength — real-study comparison plot based n≈30+n \approx 30+ per stratum far discriminating.
code: pipeline_result$plotObjects$stratifiedModels$exposure$comparisonPlots[["Malaria Preexposed_vs_Malaria Naive"]]

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: recommended production threshold averaged network 0.40. use 0.20 vignette demo dataset deliberately small.

what: 12.2 Signed strength and the delta score
title: Getting Started with networkR
text: Raw bootstrap strength non-negative. Multiplying sign(r)\text{sign}(r) gives signed strength, captures whether dependency positive negative (e.g. consistent inverse co-variation two cell types r<0r < 0 therefore negative signed strength despite potentially high bootstrap stability). delta Δ=s1−s2\Delta = s_1 - s_2 quantifies differential connectivity: Large positive Δ\Delta: edge stronger/positive Group 1. Large negative Δ\Delta: edge stronger/positive Group 2. |Δ||\Delta| near zero: edge similar across groups, absent . default labelling threshold (edgeLabelThreshold) 0.20 — edges |Δ|>0.20|\Delta| > 0.20 receive labels comparison scatter plot.

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: classify_edge_type() assigns edge comparison table one following categories: Cross-compartment edges biologically interesting encode inferred information flow tissue compartments.

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: Conditional independence ≠ causation. DAG edge →BA \B means AA direct conditional predictor BB model. Causal interpretation requires additional domain assumptions experimental validation. Small samples inflate bootstrap instability. n<20n < 20 per stratum, many edges stability < 0.30 regardless true underlying structure. Cross-compartment inference still depends missing-data handling. row now contains real paired Liver PBMC measurements subject, MICE uses genuine within-subject cross-compartment information filling assay dropouts. imputed values reflect assay failure, structurally absent tissue block. Discretization destroys signal. Three-level discretization loses continuous variation. Edges variables complex non-linear relationships may missed artificially strengthened.

title: Getting Started with networkR
text: n≈50n \approx 50+ per sub-cohort 1 000 bootstrap replicates, full pipeline run typically completes 5–15 minutes modern laptop.
code: # Production-grade overrides — replace the demo values above production_overrides <- list(   structureLearning = list(     bootstrapReplicates     = 1000,   # Friedman et al. recommend R >= 200, ideally >= 500     averageNetworkThreshold = 0.40    # Conservative; increases edge specificity   ),   imputation = list(     numberOfImputations = 5,          # Standard MICE recommendation     maximumIterations   = 20   ),   discretization = list(     initialBreaks = 20,               # Default; adequate for n >= 30     finalBreaks   = 3   ) )

what: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines for Experimental Studies
title: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines for Experimental Studies
text: networkR R package scaffold configurable Bayesian network analysis factorial repeated-measures studies. intend extend gaussian copulas/graphical models, now, just Bayesian networks.

title: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines for Experimental Studies
text: YAML programmatic configuration Panel ingestion data_sheets/ Derived design variables MICE predictive mean matching imputation Hartemink discretization fallback handling Tier-aware blacklist construction Bootstrap Bayesian network fitting bnlearn ggraph(layout = "fr") network plotting strength.plot() fallback path CI scaffolding, test scaffolding, pkgdown scaffolding

title: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines for Experimental Studies
code: library(networkR)  configuration <- ParseConfiguration("inst/example_configuration.yml") analysisResult <- RunPipeline(configuration)  analysisResult$plotObjects$fullModel

title: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines for Experimental Studies
text: repository now contains package foundation. remaining work fill specialized analyses R Markdown document, including joint paired-tissue modeling, mediation workflows, correlation reporting.

title: Bayesian network analysis helpers — AnalysisFunctions
text: Functions constructing blacklist constraints, learning Bayesian networks, comparing structure strength across cohorts.

title: Bayesian network analysis helpers — AnalysisFunctions
code: BuildBlacklist(discretizationResult, configuration)  LearnBayesianNetwork(discretizationResult, configuration)  CompareStructures(firstStructure, secondStructure, firstLabel = "First",   secondLabel = "Second")

title: Bayesian network analysis helpers — AnalysisFunctions
text: discretizationResult result DiscretizeMeasurements(). configuration networkR configuration object. firstStructure result LearnBayesianNetwork(). secondStructure result LearnBayesianNetwork(). firstLabel Label first structure. secondLabel Label second structure.

title: Bayesian network analysis helpers — AnalysisFunctions
text: BuildBlacklist() returns list containing blacklist active exogenous variables. LearnBayesianNetwork() returns list learned networks, bootstrap strength estimates, metadata. CompareStructures() returns list containing comparison table.

title: Configuration helpers — ConfigurationFunctions
text: Helpers constructing validating YAML-backed analysis configuration.

title: Configuration helpers — ConfigurationFunctions
code: BuildConfiguration(overrides = list())  ParseConfiguration(filePath)  ValidateConfiguration(configuration)

title: Configuration helpers — ConfigurationFunctions
text: overrides named list configuration overrides. filePath Path YAML configuration file. configuration networkR configuration object.

title: Configuration helpers — ConfigurationFunctions
text: BuildConfiguration() ParseConfiguration(), validated configuration object. ValidateConfiguration(), validated configuration object, invisibly.

title: Data preparation helpers — DataFunctions
text: Functions reading panel files, deriving study variables, imputing missing values, discretizing measurements Bayesian network structure learning.

title: Data preparation helpers — DataFunctions
code: ReadPanels(configuration)  AddDerivedVariables(subjectTable, configuration)  ImputeMeasurements(subjectTable, configuration)  DiscretizeMeasurements(imputationResult, configuration)

title: Data preparation helpers — DataFunctions
text: configuration networkR configuration object. subjectTable subject-level table. imputationResult result ImputeMeasurements().

title: Data preparation helpers — DataFunctions
text: ReadPanels() returns subject-level data frame. AddDerivedVariables() returns subject table derived variables. ImputeMeasurements() returns list containing imputed data analysis metadata. DiscretizeMeasurements() returns list containing continuous discrete analysis data.

title: Plot Bayesian network results — PlotFunctions
text: Helpers drawing averaged Bayesian network graphs ggraph saving plots package defaults.

title: Plot Bayesian network results — PlotFunctions
code: PlotBayesianNetwork(structureResult, configuration, title = NULL)  SavePlot(plotObject, filePath, configuration = NULL)

title: Plot Bayesian network results — PlotFunctions
text: structureResult result LearnBayesianNetwork(). configuration networkR configuration object. title Optional plot title. plotObject ggplot object. filePath Output file path.

title: Plot Bayesian network results — PlotFunctions
text: PlotBayesianNetwork() returns ggplot object. SavePlot() returns output file path, invisibly.

what: Run the networkR analysis pipeline — RunPipeline
title: Run the networkR analysis pipeline — RunPipeline
text: Runs end--end networkR analysis workflow configuration parsing structure learning plot generation.

title: Run the networkR analysis pipeline — RunPipeline
code: RunPipeline(configuration)

title: Run the networkR analysis pipeline — RunPipeline
text: configuration networkR configuration object YAML file path.

title: Run the networkR analysis pipeline — RunPipeline
text: pipeline result object containing subject table, imputation result, discretization result, full model result, generated plot objects.

what: networkR: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines — networkR-package
title: networkR: Flexible Bayesian Network Pipelines — networkR-package
text: networkR package provides configurable Bayesian network analysis workflows factorial repeated-measures studies. current scaffold supports panel ingestion, derived variable handling, predictive mean matching imputation, discretization, blacklist construction, structure learning, structure comparison, ggraph-based visualization.
